entry.title: Re: Automatic payment processing using DME for Internatio...
entry.content:
Good day @Rama21 

Thank you for your question.

Based on what SAP offers in the Public Cloud, your question would have to be addressed in two ways. Let me clarify:

First let me address your own question

Question: Is it possible in SAP Hana S4 Public cloud to automatically determine and use the correct Vendor bank account during the payment based on Currency?

Answer: The Public Cloud does not offer a feature that automatically switches among several supplier bank accounts purely by currency alone. The reason is, in Public Cloud, automatic payment processing does not support currency-based bank determination for your own house bank account, because Set Up Bank Determination for Payment Transaction (SSCUI 101045) is maintained by payment method and payment currency.

The distinction and possibilities attached to your question:

  • If your question is: Can the Public Cloud automatically choose our own paying bank account by currency?

Answer: Yes, that is standard and you configure it in SSCUI 101045 with payment method and currency.

2671294 - No Valid Payment Method Found - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4603118feda9495892abadbe8f8c3d14/3c0bb62cebd04e3bb53db9cb37ac355f.html?locale=en-US

  • If your question is: Can the Public Cloud automatically choose the correct supplier bank account only from currency when the supplier has multiple bank accounts?

Answer: That would be outside the standard rule. The safer approach for you is to maintain supplier bank details correctly and use Partner Bank Type when a specific supplier bank account must be selected.

 

Practically, I would recommend for your scenario to treat the standard design as follows:

  • Configure currency-based determination for your house bank in SSCUI 101045.
  • Use Maintain Business Partner to maintain all supplier bank accounts and use Partner Bank Type to identify the intended supplier bank account when you need to.

Here are references for more guidance

3444334 - Error "Customer/vendor does not have suitable bank details for payment method &" - SAP for Me

2671294 - No Valid Payment Method Found - SAP for Me

 

The next step is to verify two things separately in your tenant: First, that the correct house bank is being selected for each currency in the payment proposal, and second, that the supplier has all bank details maintained in Automatic Payment Transactions with clear Partner Bank Type values.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Appikatla Venkata Murali Krishna
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Appikatla Venkata Murali krishna, and I am currently an aspiring SAP MM Consultant. I have completed my studies and have been actively building my skills in SAP S/4HANA with a focus on Materials Management (MM).

I have worked on real-time scenarios like procurement processes, freight vendor integration, and inventory management, which helped me understand how businesses manage materials efficiently using SAP. I am passionate about learning new SAP technologies and improving my functional knowledge to grow in this field.

As part of the Hello SAP Community Initiative, I’m excited to share my experience. You can read more about the initiative in Hello SAP Community Initiative , which gives the full background and context.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become a successful SAP MM Consultant working on global implementation projects. I aim to contribute to organizations by optimizing procurement processes and improving supply chain efficiency using SAP solutions.

What do you imagine is the future of your topic (SAP, AI, Cloud)?

I believe the future of SAP lies in the deep integration of AI and Cloud within business processes, especially in areas like procurement and supply chain management. SAP S/4HANA Cloud, combined with Generative AI and predictive analytics, will transform how businesses make decisions.

For example, in a procurement scenario, instead of manually creating purchase requisitions based on stock levels, AI can automatically:

  • Predict material demand using historical data
  • Suggest the best vendor based on price, delivery performance, and past history
  • Auto-create purchase requisitions and even purchase orders
  • Detect anomalies such as unusual pricing or duplicate invoices

In SAP MM, this means processes like inventory management, vendor selection, and invoice verification will become more intelligent and automated. AI can also help in physical inventory by identifying discrepancies and suggesting corrections in real time.

Generative AI will further enhance user experience by allowing users to interact with SAP systems using natural language. For example, a user could simply ask:

"Show me the materials with low stock and create a purchase order for the best vendor" — and the system can execute it.

Overall, the future will shift from manual execution to intelligent decision-making, where SAP professionals will focus more on process optimization and strategy rather than routine tasks.

What is your hobby?

My hobbies include learning new technologies, exploring SAP scenarios, and improving my technical knowledge. I also enjoy watching tech-related content and staying updated with industry trends.

What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

One of the most interesting projects I worked on was Freight Vendor Integration in SAP MM. In this project, I learned how to configure condition types like FRB1, manage multiple vendors (material and freight), and handle invoice verification. It was fun because it connected real-world logistics with SAP configuration.

What do you enjoy most in your studies/work and why?

What I enjoy most is understanding real-time business processes and configuring them in SAP. It is exciting to see how a business requirement is translated into a system solution. Especially in SAP MM, I enjoy working on purchase orders, inventory, and vendor management.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

During SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025, I gained valuable insights into how SAP is evolving with modern technologies. Some of my key learnings include:

  • SAP is strongly focusing on AI-driven automation, especially with tools like SAP Joule (AI Copilot), which helps users perform tasks faster using natural language
  • Learned how SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) plays a key role in integrating AI, data, and analytics across different modules
  • Understood the importance of clean core strategy in S/4HANA for better system performance and easier upgrades
  • Saw real-world use cases where AI is used in procurement to automate vendor selection and improve decision-making
  • Gained awareness of how cloud-based SAP solutions are becoming the standard for future implementations

One important takeaway for me was that future SAP consultants need not only functional knowledge but also an understanding of AI and cloud technologies. This motivated me to continue learning and upgrading my skills to stay relevant in the evolving SAP ecosystem.

And now let’s hear from you!

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

I would love to hear your thoughts and experiences please share them in the comments!

entry.title: Hello SAP community my name is Vishwa Bharadhwaj S P I'm excited to start my journey as a student
entry.content:
Hello SAP community, my name is Vishwa Bharadhwaj S P. I’m currently a student, and over time I’ve been exploring my interest in technology and trying to understand how things work beyond just what’s taught in class. My journey as a student hasn’t been perfect, but it has helped me realize the importance of learning on my own and improving step by step.  I spend time building my knowledge through different resources and trying to get better at what I do. I’m still figuring things out, but I’m focused on growing and building a strong career over time.

Here are the five questions I would like to answer as a a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative 

1. What is your professional life dream?

I want to build a career in technology where I’m actually useful, not just doing tasks for the sake of it. I’ve always been curious about how systems work, so I want to be in a place where I can solve real problems and see the impact of what I’m doing. I don’t have everything figured out yet, but I know I want to keep learning, get better at what I do, and eventually reach a level where people trust my work.

2. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

If I’m being honest, I don’t have a perfect picture. But I do see myself more confident and experienced than I am now. I want to be working on real projects, not just learning basics, and actually understanding what I’m doing instead of guessing. I’d like to be in a role where I’m improving consistently and not feeling stuck.

3. What is your hobby?

Most of the time, I end up exploring new technologies or learning something online. It’s not always planned, I just go down random topics and try to understand how things work. I also play chess quite a bit. It helps me slow down and think properly instead of rushing decisions. Sometimes I lose badly, but that’s part of it. Going to the gym is another thing I’ve been consistent with. It clears my head. It’s one of the few places where progress feels straightforward, you show up, you work, and over time you see change.

4. Which 4 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

I want to build a career where I feel stable and know what I’m doing.
I want to keep learning instead of becoming comfortable and stagnant.
I want to do something useful with what I learn, not just work for a salary.
I want to reach a point where my journey makes sense to someone else who’s trying to figure things out.

5. What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

They didn’t really sit me down and explain big life lessons, but I’ve seen how they handled things. They kept going even when things weren’t easy. That stuck with me. So for me, the lesson is simple, keep doing the work even when you don’t feel like it. Don’t depend too much on motivation. Just stay consistent and things slowly start making sense.

6.How do you choose which area to focus on in SAP as a student?

 I think it depends on your background. Since I come from mechanical engineering, I find SAP Material Management more relatable because it connects with materials, inventory, and supply processes that I’ve already studied in theory. SAP MM feels like digital side of the same process where everything is organized, tracked, planned and optimized

My SAP Inside Track 2026 Experience 

Attended SAP Inside Track 2026 with my friends, and honestly that made the experience better. We discussed a lot after each session, and it helped me understand things more clearly instead of just listening and moving on. I had great insights on Gen Ai and Applied research and the clarity of the content was perfect.

This was inside the SAP Labs Innovation Park. Seeing the model and how everything is structured gave me a better idea of how large-scale systems and environments are planned. It felt more practical than just learning concepts in theory.

The campus itself left an impression. It did not feel like a typical place, it felt like a space where actual innovation happens. Being there made things feel more real and gave me a better sense of where I want to reach.

Now let's hear it from you.

One thing I'm still thinking about after the session is

How are AI agents being practically integrated into enterprise systems, and what challenges come up during implementation?

In the configuration step for 'Define Consolidation Master Data Fields' you have if GLAccount should be aggregated on carryforward. If you have this setting enabled you will lose GLAccount when creating the opening balance.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community My Name Is Nidhi N H and I'm excited to begin my jorney here
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!
My name is Nidhi N H, a Mechanical Engineering student at REVA University in Bangalore, India.

 Our SAP journey started as something completely new for us. At first, we didn’t have much idea about what SAP actually is or how it works. It felt confusing because it was different from what we were used to learning. But slowly, our curiosity increased, and we decided to explore it step by step.

I am very happy to be part of the Hello SAP Community initiative. This opportunity allows beginners like me to explore the SAP ecosystem and connect with professionals around the world.
As a student, I am interested in learning new technologies and understanding how SAP solutions help businesses manage their operations efficiently. Through the SAP Community, I hope to learn from experts, discover new ideas, and grow my professional network.

I look forward to sharing my learning journey and contributing to the SAP Community in the future.

 

Thank you SAP Community for this wonderful opportunity!

Here are some exciting questions I've chosen to answer as a part of the Hello SAP Community. initiative;

1.what is your professional life dream ?

My professional dream is to build a successful career in technology and engineering. I want to continuously learn new skills and work on innovative solutions that help organizations improve their efficiency. I also aim to contribute to the tech community and grow as a professional in the global technology ecosystem.

2.where do you see yourself in 5 years ?

In the next five years, I see myself as a skilled professional working in the technology industry. I hope to gain strong knowledge, practical experience, and work on real-world projects. I also want to keep learning and improving myself while contributing positively to my organization and community.

3.what is your hobby ?

One of my hobbies is learning about new technologies and exploring new ideas related to engineering and innovation. I also enjoy reading, learning new skills online, and improving my knowledge through different educational platforms.

4. which 5 thing do you absolutely want to achives in life ?

There are several goals I want to achieve in my life:

Build a successful professional career.
Continuously learn and develop new skills.
Contribute to society through knowledge and innovation.
Inspire others through my journey and experiences.

5.what is the best lesson your parents taught you ?

The best lesson my parents taught me is the importance of hard work, honesty, and patience. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals and never give up during difficult situations. These values continue to guide me in my education and future career.

6. How do you see yourself helping society with SAP knowledge?
With SAP knowledge, I aim to contribute by improving efficiency in industries such as manufacturing and agriculture. By using ERP systems, I can help organizations optimize resources, reduce waste, and improve productivity. This indirectly benefits society by supporting sustainable development and better resource management.

With the knowledge I gain, I aim to develop practical and affordable solutions for real-world problems. For example, I want to work on smart agriculture systems like weed detection and automated irrigation to support farmers. I also hope to contribute to sustainable technologies that reduce environmental impact. By applying my skills, I want to make technology more accessible and useful, especially for rural communities.

7. How will SAP help your career?
SAP will enhance my profile by adding in-demand skills and give me exposure to enterprise-level systems, which are highly valued in the job market.

8. How will you apply SAP knowledge in your field?
As a mechanical engineering student, I can use SAP in areas like production planning, supply chain management, and inventory control to improve efficiency in manufacturing industries.

 

My Experience at SAP TechEd Bangalore.

Recently, I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore, and it was an amazing learning experience for me. As a student interested in technology and innovation, being part of such a global tech event was truly inspiring. One of the most exciting parts of the event was exploring the Demo Pods, where SAP experts explained different technologies and solutions. It was great to see how SAP technologies like BTP are used to solve real-world business problems.

Key Takeaways from the event.
Generative AI is essential now – Tools like SAP Joule show that AI isn’t just an add-on anymore; it’s becoming the core of productivity and automation.
Data drives sustainability – Using BTP for ESG reporting shows how data can turn sustainability goals into real, measurable impact.
Across every theme at TechEd, Al, automation, integration, and developer enablement, one thing stood out: data readiness is the true enabler of transformation. Without it, even the most advanced tools can't deliver their full value.

I had the wonderful opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore along with friends from my college. It was a great experience for all of us to be part of such a global technology event. Our college group explored different sessions, demo pods, and interacted with SAP experts. We also had the chance to connect with members of the SAP Community and learn about new technologies like SAP BTP, AI, and cloud innovations.


Now, I would like to finish the post by asking you all a question which you're invited to answer in the comments section:
What is a "mind-blowing" and fascinating technology you expect to be mainstream by 2030??
“I’d love to hear your voice in this conversation—share your ideas, insights, or predictions in the comments. Your perspectives will enrich this dialogue, and I’m genuinely excited to learn from the thoughts you bring to the table.”

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! 👋 – I’m Sanjay, and This Is My Journey

My name is Sanjay M N, and I’m currently pursuing Mechanical Engineering at REVA University in Bangalore, India.

I’m truly excited to be part of the Hello SAP Community initiative. For a beginner like me, this is a great opportunity to explore the SAP ecosystem and connect with professionals across the world.

As a student from a core engineering background, I’ve always been curious about how technology connects with real-world industries. This curiosity is what led me to explore SAP and understand how it helps organizations manage large-scale operations efficiently.

I’m also excited to share my learning journey and contribute back to the SAP Community as I grow.

Thank you, SAP Community, for this wonderful opportunity! 🙌

🌱A Little About Me

1. What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to build a stable and meaningful career. I have a strong interest in railways, and I’m planning to prepare for the RRB exam to become a Junior Engineer. At the same time, I’m also exploring technology like SAP because I want to keep learning and stay open to new opportunities. I’m curious to understand how systems like SAP are used behind the scenes to manage large-scale operations like railways.

2. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In 5 years, I see myself in a responsible position, hopefully working in the railways as a Junior Engineer or in a role where I can apply my mechanical knowledge. I want to be someone who is confident in their skills and keeps improving with time.

3. What is your hobby?

I enjoy activities like swimming, cycling, and reading books. These help me relax and stay balanced. I also like exploring new things and learning online whenever I get time.

4. What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

One thing my parents always taught me is to stay strong during tough times and not lose hope. No matter how difficult the situation is, they believe that if we stay patient and keep putting in effort, things will eventually get better. This mindset has really shaped how I handle challenges.

5. What do you imagine is the future of your topic?

I feel the future will be a combination of engineering and technologies like AI and cloud. Even in industries like railways and manufacturing, systems will become smarter with automation and data. I believe platforms like SAP will play an important role in managing and improving these systems, and I’m interested in learning more about it.

🚀My Experience at SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025

Recently, I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore, and it was truly an inspiring experience. As a student, being part of such a global tech event gave me a completely new perspective on how technology is shaping industries.

One of the most exciting parts was exploring the Demo Pods, where SAP experts demonstrated how technologies like SAP BTP are used to solve real-world business challenges.

🌟A Memorable Interaction

One of the most inspiring moments for me was interacting with Moshe Naveh during the SAP EdTech Tour 2025.

He explained how the SAP Community works and how it connects people from different parts of the world. What really stayed with me was his advice—that by being active in this community, we can interact with industry professionals, learn from their experiences, and grow together.

As a beginner, that conversation was truly motivating. It made me realize that learning is not just about courses or content, but also about engaging with the right community.

🚀 My Learning & Experiences

 

Other than SAP TechEd Bangalore event I also had the chance to attend the SAP Insight Track (SIT) at the SAP Labs India Innovation Park, Devanahalli. It was another great experience where I attended multiple sessions and got exposed to different ideas and technologies within SAP.

Along with this, I recently completed a certification course on SAP Procurement Process Overview, which helped me understand the basics of how procurement works in SAP systems.

🤝The Best Part: The Community

While the sessions and technologies were impressive, the most valuable part of the experience was the people.

Connecting with SAP experts, interacting with community members, and attending the event with my college friends made the experience even more meaningful. These interactions are where real learning happens.

💬 Let’s Connect

I would love to hear your thoughts on this & learn from this community...

👉“As a Mechanical Engineering student, how can I align my background with SAP, especially with the growing role of AI in industries?”
👉“Also, how can I make the most out of the SAP Community to accelerate my learning?”

This is just the beginning of my journey, and I’m excited to learn, connect, and grow with this amazing community.

entry.title: Discover How to Use SAP Road Map Explorer in Five Steps
entry.content:
Discover How to Use SAP Road Map Explorer in Five Steps

Take a first look to view how the interactive SAP Road Map Explorer tool can help you navigate the complexities of planning for the future. Using the tool, you can:

Create your own road map: In just a few clicks, the interactive tool showcases recent and future innovations (or product features). You can create and tailor your own road map, filtering innovations from SAP solutions, industries, business processes, and strategic topics and even focus topics such as Artificial Intelligence and Digital Transformation. Use filters to help narrow or expand your road map search.

Make informed decisions: Using the tool, you can gain a more holistic view of each innovation, making it easier to come to informed decisions. Planned innovations have an overview and benefits section, while delivered innovations have additional information such as specific features and a technical view section. Stakeholders from business management to IT teams can all be part of the conversation.

Collaborate with colleagues: The collaboration continues with the tool’s “Save and Share” features, making it easy to share a specific innovation or a whole road map with other colleagues. The “My Road Maps” section lets you view and share your saved road maps and innovations once you log back in. Additionally, you can download road maps to your desktop to view them offline. Create and collaborate with ease with SAP Road Map Explorer.

To first access the tool, log in with your S-user credentials or create an account for free on roadmaps.sap.com. Then, simply follow the steps below to start exploring.

Step 1: Search for your topic

There are four ways you can view a road map (see Figure 1):

    • Browse on the top according to a product, industry, business process or strategic topic.

 

    • Use the search bar to type the topic of your choice.

 

    • If you have not decided which specific road map you are looking for, browse “Explore SAP Road Maps.”

 

 

 

 

Figure 1: Viewing Road Maps in SAP Road Map Explorer



Step 2: Customize with filters

On top of the Kanban board, there are four categories for you to choose from: products, processes, industries and focus topics. Numbers on the right side of filters indicate how many innovations will appear if you choose that filter (see Figure 2). Filters will only appear if there are innovations available to show, so you’ll never experience zero results.

 

Figure 2: Customizing Your Search in SAP Road Map Explorer



Step 3: Explore innovations on the Kanban board

The Kanban board is the page that showcases all the innovations (or product features) in quarters. Use the left or right scrolls to browse through the road map. Each quarter column has an arrow on the top right – click to change your view preferences of the innovations by collapsing or expanding the innovation cards (see Figure 3).

 

Figure 3: Exploring Innovations

Click each innovation card to view more details such as the innovation description and benefits. Planned innovations will have additional information such as features and the technical view (see Figure 4).

Tip: Use the “star” button to favorite certain innovations that stand out to you. This makes it easier to spot innovations on the Kanban board.

 

Figure 4: Additional Information on Innovations

 


Step 4: Share with your colleagues

The top right of each innovation card has a share button, giving you a link to copy and paste. Once you save your road map or innovation, a link with the url to share is copied to the clipboard (see Figure 5).

 

Figure 5: Sharing Road Maps

 

Step #5: Save and download

The top right corner of the Kanban board has a “Save” button, which allows you to save the road map you created with the selected filters. Easily access them later under “My Road Maps.” If you want to save certain innovations, click on the “Add to Collection” button on the innovation card to either add it to an existing collection or create a new collection (see Figure 6). You can access your collections under “My Road Maps”.

Lastly, a download button is available, which will automatically produce a Microsoft Excel file with all innovations pertaining to the road map you created.

 

 

Figure 6: Saving Innovations

 


 

You can start exploring now at roadmaps.sap.com.  

 

 

Labels

  • SAP Road Map Explorer
  • industryroadmaps
  • productroadmaps
  • roadmapexplorer
  • roadmaps

Add tags

entry.title: Re: Centralized credit limit for specific customers
entry.content:
Maintain the credit limit centrally on Reliance Mumbai BP (with UKM000 role) and assign all branch BPs to this HQ BP as their credit account in Credit Management. This way, all branch transactions roll up to the HQ’s credit limit.

To achieve your requirement, perform the below steps

1) Assign Credit Management Role (UKM000)
Open the Manage Business Partner Master Data app. For Reliance Mumbai HQ BP, assign the role UKM000 – Credit Management. Maintain the credit segment and credit limit here.

2) Maintain Credit Limit
In the same role, enter the credit segment (usually by company code/country) and the credit limit amount. This HQ BP becomes the “credit account.”

3) Link Branch BPs to HQ BP
For each branch BP (Pune, Delhi, Ahmedabad, Chennai), go into their BP master data. In the Credit Management assignment, set the Credit Account BP field to point to Reliance Mumbai HQ BP. This ensures that when you create sales orders for branch BPs, the system checks credit against the HQ BP’s limit.

entry.title: Hello SAP community! My name is B C NIKHIL and I'm excited to start my journey.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community

My name is B C NIKHIL and I’m currently pursuing Mechanical Engineering at REVA University in Bangalore, India.

I am very happy to be part of the Hello SAP Community initiative. This opportunity allows beginners like me to explore the SAP ecosystem and connect with professionals around the world.

As a student, I am interested in learning new technologies and understanding how SAP solutions help businesses manage their operations efficiently. Through the SAP Community, I hope to learn from experts, discover new ideas, and grow my professional network.

I look forward to sharing my learning journey and contributing to the  SAP  Community in the future.

Thank you SAP Community for this wonderful opportunity!

Here are the questions I chose to answer as a part of the initiative:

1. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In five years, I envision myself as a Senior Thermal Design Engineer or a CFD Specialist. Having transitioned from my studies at REVA University into the professional world, I see myself leading complex simulations for high-performance thermal systems perhaps in the electric vehicle (EV) sector or aerospace—ensuring that cutting-edge technology runs efficiently and safely.

2. What is your hobby?

One of my favorite ways to spend time outside of engineering is following IPL cricket, specifically cheering for Royal Challengers Bangalore (RCB). There is a certain "engineering" to the game the strategy, the physics of a fast bowl, and the data analytics involved that makes it incredibly engaging to watch. It’s the perfect high-energy balance to the focus required for academic research.

3. If you could be a superhero, who would you be? What do you enjoy most in your studies and why?

I’d choose Iron Man (Tony Stark). Beyond the suit, I admire the way he uses engineering to solve seemingly impossible global problems. In my own studies, what I enjoy most is Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). There is something fascinating about taking invisible forces, like heat and airflow, and using math and software to make them visible and predictable. It feels like having a "superpower" to see how the physical world works at a molecular level.

4. What do you imagine is the future of your topic?

The future of Thermal Sciences and Mechanical Design lies in the "Digital Twin" revolution. I predict we will move toward a world where every physical machine from a tiny drone motor to a massive power plant has a real-time AI counterpart. We won't just design a part and hope it works; we will use generative design and AI-driven fluid analysis to create organic, ultra-efficient shapes that humans could never manually draft, potentially reducing energy waste by half globally.

5. What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

The most rewarding project was designing the secondary belt drive for an electric scooter. It required balancing very specific constraints, like a high reduction ratio and fixed pulley diameters. It was "fun" because it bridged the gap between textbook theory and a real-world machine you see on the streets every day. Seeing the math translate into a functional drivetrain that powers a vehicle is exactly why I chose this field.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025 

 


My Experience at SAP TechEd Bangalore

Recently, I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore, and it was an amazing learning experience for me. As a student interested in technology and innovation, being part of such a global tech event was truly inspiring. One of the most exciting parts of the event was exploring the Demo Pods, where SAP experts explained different technologies and solutions. It was great to see how SAP technologies like BTP are used to solve real-world business problems.

Key Takeaways & Highlights
While there was a lot to take in, these three themes defined the event for me:

The Power of Generative AI: The evolution of SAP Joule was front and center. It’s clear that AI is no longer a "plus-one" feature but the core engine driving developer productivity and business process automation.

A "Clean Core" is Non-Negotiable: There was a significant focus on the Clean Core Strategy. Learning about new extensibility patterns reinforced that staying close to the standard is the only way to ensure long-term agility and easier upgrades.

Driving Sustainability with Data: I was particularly inspired by the sessions on ESG reporting. Seeing how we can leverage BTP to turn sustainability goals into measurable data points is a game-changer for the modern enterprise.

The Best Part of SAP TechEd:

The Community  While the product announcements and technical roadmaps are what bring us to SAP TechEd Bangalore, it’s the people who make the experience unforgettable. One of my personal highlights was the chance to connect directly with fellow community members and SAP experts on the show floor. Whether we were discussing the nuances of BTP at the Demo Pods or simply sharing our learning journeys, these interactions are where the real "tech magic" happens.

 

SAP TechEd Bangalore – A Great Learning Experience with My College Team:

 


I had the wonderful opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore along with friends from my college. It was a great experience for all of us to be part of such a global technology event. Our college group explored different sessions, demo pods, and interacted with SAP experts. We also had the chance to connect with members of the SAP Community and learn about new technologies like SAP BTP, AI, and cloud innovations.

Learning journey:

 

The SAP Procurement Process provides a structured framework for managing the end-to-end lifecycle of material acquisition, ensuring seamless integration between departmental needs and vendor fulfillment. My training in this module covered the critical stages of the Procure-to-Pay (P2P) cycle, beginning with the strategic determination of requirements through Purchase Requisitions and moving into source selection via RFQs. By mastering the creation and monitoring of Purchase Orders, I gained a deep understanding of how standardized procurement documentation maintains data integrity and legal compliance within a corporate environment.

The latter phase of the workflow focuses on the logistical and financial reconciliation essential for operational accuracy. I am proficient in handling Goods Receipts—specifically understanding movement types and their impact on real-time inventory levels—and the vital Three-Way Match during Invoice Verification. This ensures that the Purchase Order, physical delivery, and vendor billing are perfectly aligned before payment. This expertise allows for the reduction of discrepancies, optimized stock management, and a streamlined flow of information across an organization’s supply chain.

Through this course, I have acquired the technical proficiency to manage the full SAP procurement cycle, focusing on inventory optimization, rigorous financial reconciliation, and the strategic management of supplier relationships.

Now, I would like to finish the post by asking you all a question which you're invited to answer in the comments section:

Which SAP modules offer the best career growth for beginners?

entry.title: Collision Handling between Absences and Attendances in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking - 1H 2026
entry.content:
Introduction

I am delighted to announce that with the 1H 2026 release, we are delivering one of the most awaited enhancements in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking—the ability to handle collisions between absences and attendances when both records overlap with each other. With this solution, you as a customer or an implementation consultant can define how you want the system to react when such collisions occur.

And yes, we are also closing on some of the highly voted Influence Ideas - Hurray!

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/271503 - 31 votes

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/301525 - 46 votes

In real-life business scenarios, employees don’t always work strictly outside of their absence periods. Late clock‑outs or early clock-ins, emergency on‑call duties, or overtime during planned leave are common situations that challenge traditional time recording rules.

Until recently, such situations frequently resulted in collision errors, failed time imports, or manual rework by administrators. With the Collision Resolution for Absences  vs. Attendances feature, SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking now provides the flexibility to handle these overlaps in a controlled and consistent way.

In this blog, I’ll explain when and why this feature is needed, walk through some business use cases, share the integration aspects with time valuation and payroll and show how to configure collision handling effectively.

 

Business Context

The Collision Resolution feature for Absences and Attendances extends the existing absence‑to‑absence collision framework. It allows you to define how the system should behave when absences overlap with attendance or on‑call records.

From my experience, this becomes especially important in environments where:

  • Clock-in Clock-out data is imported from external systems
  • Late clock‑outs are operationally unavoidable
  • On‑call or overtime work happens even on absence days

In these situations, blocking time records outright does not reflect real business needs and often results in manual corrections and frustrated users. With the new collision handling, the system instead evaluates configurable rules to determine whether an overlap should be allowed or rejected - giving customers and consultants much better control over collision behaviour in Time Management.

Here’s a sneak peak of how the feature works in action 😊

How to Configure?

In this section, I’ll walk through how to configure collision handling step by step. From my experience, following this sequence avoids most configuration and recalculation issues later.

Before you start configuring collision handling between absences and attendances, make sure the following prerequisites are met:

  • Time Tracking must be enabled in your test environment.
  • Verify that you can see the new section under the “Time Management Collision Resolution” object in Manage Data.
  • If this section is not visible, one of the following is likely the reason:
    • Time Tracking is not enabled, or
    • The nightly job TLMTriggerTask has not yet run, which is required to activate and expose this configuration section in the system.

Step 1: Create Time Type Collision Groupings

First, you need to define collision groupings for the relevant time types.

Navigate to Manage Data → Time Type Collision Grouping object to define groupings.

Here, create new collision groupings for the required categories:

  • Absence

Tip: Absence category was already supported with absence vs absence collisions. You can also reuse an existing collision grouping that is already assigned to an absence time type.

  • Attendance
  • On‑Call

Note:
All existing Time Type Collision Groupings are defaulted and migrated to Absence category. You must explicitly create groupings if you want to use them for Attendance or On‑Call.

This is how you can create the time type collision grouping for Category “Attendance”:

This is the time type collision grouping for Category “On-Call”

(Screenshots: Time Type Collision Grouping creation with category Attendance / On‑Call)

Note:
You can also create Time Type Collision Groupings for Attendance or On‑Call time types that are country‑specific. This allows you to enable and use this feature only for selected time types in specific countries, giving you more flexibility to address local business or legal requirements without impacting global configurations.

Step 2: Assign Collision Groupings to Time Types

Once the collision groupings are created, they need to be assigned to the respective time types.

You need to go to the Time Type Object in Manage Data.

  • Time Type object (for Absence, Attendance, or On‑Call)

Important rules:

  • Collision groupings can only be assigned to time types with classification:
    • Absence
    • Attendance
    • On‑Call
  • Extra and Allowance time types cannot be configured here.
  • The country of the time type and collision grouping must match.
  • The category of the collision grouping must align with the time type classification

 

        

(Screenshot: Time Type configuration showing assigned collision grouping)               

Step 3: Configure Collision Resolution Rules

This is the most critical configuration step.

  • Go to Manage Data → Time Management Collision Resolution
  • Scroll to the section Absence and Attendance Collision Resolution.

Here, you define how overlaps should be handled when one time record already exists, and another one is created.

For each rule:

  1. Select the Incoming Time Type Collision Grouping
  2. Select the Existing Time Type Collision Grouping
  3. Choose the Collision Resolution Action:
    • Allow Concurrent, or
    • Allow Outside Absence Hours
  4. Decide whether the warning message should be displayed or hidden on:
    • Time Off UI(incoming grouping is an absence group)
    • Time Sheet UI(incoming grouping is an attendance/on-call group)

One important considerationand recommendation is that collision resolution should always be set up bidirectionally.

That means:

  • Absence → Attendance
  • Attendance → Absence

…and both with the same collision action.

This ensures consistent behaviour no matter which record is created first—whether manually, via import, or during recalculation.

You can decide independently whether warning messages are shown or hidden on the respective UIs.

The below example shows the configuration in bidirection:

(Screenshot: Time Management Collision Resolution – Absence vs Attendance section)

The system also displays warning message if the configuration is not bidirectional.

Breaks Handling:

Breaks are handled implicitly as part of the collision resolution between Absences and Attendances. Once an Attendance time type is configured with either Allow Concurrent or Allow Outside Absence Hours as the Collision Resolution Action, the system automatically considers breaks for overlap with absences.

This means you do not need to configure break‑specific collision rules separately—the behavior is derived from the attendance or on‑call collision configuration.

Important Notes on Break Behavior

There are a few important rules to keep in mind when it comes to break handling:

  • Breaks are only allowed to overlap with an absence if:

    • An Attendance or On‑Call record exists, and
    • A collision resolution configuration exists using a Time Type Collision Grouping of category Attendance or On‑Call

  • If only a break exists, and an absence is created overlapping with that break, a collision error is raised, just as in the standard system behavior.

  • On the Time Sheet side, if only an absence exists, a manual break can still be recorded, provided that a collision resolution configuration exists between Absences and Attendances.

In other words, breaks are treated as dependent records—they rely on the presence and configuration of attendance or on‑call records to determine whether overlaps with absences are allowed.

From my experience, this implicit handling of breaks helps avoid over‑configuration while still ensuring data consistency. It also prevents unintended scenarios where breaks exist independently without valid working time, while still allowing realistic recording behavior on the time sheet when supported by proper collision rules.

Here's how the time records look together with any fixed or generated breaks. 

Be Careful with Recalculation and Historical Data

Changing collision groupings or collision resolution rules does not automatically recalculate existing time sheets or absences. Since this object is not effective-dated, any changes to the configuration applies to all historical records.

I would recommend:

  • Setting an Earliest Recalculation Date if you don’t want changes to affect historical records
  • Reviewing existing data if configuration changes are applied retroactively

Tip: If you have tested collision handling and later deleted the configuration, your time sheet UI can start showing errors due to recalculation. In that case, it is recommended to also delete the time records before deleting the configuration. Please do not create support tickets for such issues 🙂 .

Limitations to Keep in Mind:

While the collision handling feature provides much‑needed flexibility, there are a few important limitations that you should be aware of when designing your configuration.

1. “Allow Outside Absence Hours” Is Not Supported for Certain Absence Types

The Allow Outside Absence Hours collision resolution action is not supported for the following absence types:

  • LOA (Leave of Absence) time types
  • Undetermined End Date time types

Important to note:

  • This validation happens at runtime, not during configuration.
  • If you configure this scenario, the system does not prevent you from saving the setup.
  • However, creating the absence record will result in a collision error.

There is one special case to be aware of:

  • You can create the absence first and then record the time sheet entry later.
  • But any subsequent edits to the absence or any recalculation of absences will fail with a collision error.

This scenario is not supported in both directions.

Reason for this restriction:
We added this limitation primarily due to performance considerations. For long‑running absences, the system needs to evaluate each individual absence day against existing time sheet records. For LOA and undetermined end‑date absences, the system does not explicitly evaluate daily time calendar entries, which would result in significant performance impact.

2.  Combining “Split and Delimit” with “Allow Concurrent”

Be cautious when combining the following collision behaviours:

  • Split and Delimit (absence vs. absence), and
  • Allow Concurrent (absence vs. attendance)

Using these configurations together for same absence types can lead to denied collisions, meaning the incoming record cannot be created.

Example: If attendance is incoming and sick leave is incoming at the same time, and there is an existing vacation, then the sick leave is created but the attendance cannot be created. This is an edge case.                                                 

 

Business Use Cases Walkthrough

Now, let’s look at some of the business use cases in detail:

Attendance with Overlapping Absences

One of the most common issues I see is a late clock‑out overlapping with a half‑day absence.

Scenario: An employee requests a half‑day absence starting at 12:15, but due to a handover or last‑minute task, the actual clock‑out happens at 12:45. This creates a small overlap between attendance and absence.

Previously, such overlaps often caused:

  • Time recording errors
  • Failed CICO imports
  • Manual corrections by admins

With Allow Concurrent configured:

  • The attendance from 08:00–12:45 is accepted
  • The absence from 12:15–17:00 remains intact
  • No error is triggered

This is particularly useful in manufacturing, healthcare, and shift‑based industries, where exact end times are not always predictable.

Configuration Used:

Here, the attendance record “Working Time” is incoming, warning message is shown on the time sheet UI. If you do not want the employees to see any warning messages, you can choose the “Hide” option in the above configuration.

(Screenshot: Attendance overlapping with existing second half‑day absence saved successfully with a warning message)

If Absence is the incoming record, warning message is displayed on the absence UI as shown:

(Screenshot: Incoming absence on second half-day overlapping with existing attendance created successfully with warning message)

External Time Clock Imports without collision errors

Another practical use case comes up frequently in customer landscapes with external time recording systems.

Scenario

  • Absence is approved in SAP SuccessFactors
  • Clock‑in/clock‑out data is imported from a third‑party system
  • Minor overlaps occur because the external system is unaware of the absence

Without collision handling, these imports can fail or generate errors.
With proper collision resolution in place, the system will significantly reduce import failures and manual clean‑up.

Make sure that the CICO is configured properly and the working time time type has the collision grouping assigned.

Any CICO events recorded in external system and imported into the SuccessFactors Time Tracking system will go through the collision resolution configuration and allow the overlaps. Warning message shows that collision has been resolved as shown below:

(Screenshot: Imported CICO records overlapping with first-half absence)

On‑Call or Overtime After a Full‑Day Absence

A very realistic scenario—especially in IT and support organizations—is on‑call work during vacations.

Scenario

  • Employee has a full‑day vacation from 08:00–17:00
  • An emergency requires on‑call work from 18:00–21:00

With Allow Outside Absence Hours:

  • The full‑day absence remains unchanged
  • On‑call time can be recorded after planned working hours
  • Overlap with absence hours is prevented

This ensures compliance while still supporting business‑critical work.

If you have a requirement where you do not want to allow overlaps but only record times outside the absence hours, use this configuration option.

Configuration Used:

As you can see in the screenshot below, on-calls can be recorded outside the full day absence from 08:00 am – 5:00 pm. This is impressive 😊.

(Screenshot: On‑call recorded after full‑day absence)

On‑Call Duties on Non‑Working Days During Absence Periods

I often get questions about multi‑day absences that span weekends or non‑working days.

Scenario

  • Absence runs from Friday to Monday
  • Saturday and Sunday are non‑working days
  • Employee performs on‑call duty on Sunday

With Allow Outside Absence Hours, on‑call can be recorded at any time on those non‑working days, because the absence does not have defined start or end times there.

There’s an absence from Friday 17th April to Monday 20th April as seen:

Now, let’s create an on-call on Saturday 18th April where an absence exists on the non-working day. This on-call can be recorded at any time of the day. The inside-outside logic doesn’t apply to absences on non-working days as they are typically without any start or end times.

(Screenshot: On‑call recorded on non‑working day during absence)

Partial Work During Sick Leave or Gradual Return

In some Nordic countries such as Norway and Sweden, employees gradually return to work while still officially on long term sick leave. This is an important use case which can work well with collision handling configuration without requiring to change the work schedules.

Scenario

  • Employee is on partial sickness; however, the sickness is recorded as a long-term multiday sickness with a partial work capacity percentage.
  • Works a few hours for knowledge transfer or light duties.
  • Attendance overlaps with absence on the same day.

With Allow Concurrent, both records are allowed to coexist, ensuring:

  • Accurate reporting of worked hours.
  • Correct leave consumption with the usage of work capacity percentage defined when creating sick leaves.
  • Compliance with local regulations.

For Norway, configure either the Work Capacity Percentage field or the Work Incapacity Percentage field based on your business requirements:

  • Use Work Capacity Percentage if you integrate with SAP Payroll. The Work Capacity Percentage field is replicated to payroll IT2001 for standard payroll processing.
  • Use Work Incapacity Percentage if you import absence data directly from government files (NAV) if you are not using SAP HCM on-premise Payroll or SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll.

Here’s an example of absence creation with the work capacity percentage field in Norway:


Note: The absence creation with work capacity is available with Employee Central Time Off for Norway and Sweden

Whereas for Sweden, it’s the opposite. The work incapacity percentage is replicated to IT2001 in SAP HCM Payroll system:

Tip: The idea is that you do not need to maintain work schedules or temporary changes as per the employee’s working times. With this approach, you can allow employees to create attendances overlapping with the long-term sick leaves such that they are paid correctly for the hours recorded. The absence hours are calculated in Payroll with the use of work capacity/incapacity percentage fields. The attendance hours are evaluated using time valuation and sent to payroll.

I like it very much that using collision handling, you can achieve this long standing gap of partial sickness and attendance recording too! 🙂

Configuration used to allow overlaps:

In this scenario, I have hidden the warning message to be displayed to the end-user as they just want to record attendances as they work during their recovery period from sickness.

There’s a long-term sickness in my example from May 1st to June 30th:

Now, let’s check the attendance recordings overlapping with the absence hours. As you can see, no warning message is displayed on the UI. Employee can record attendances freely without any fixed start or end times.

Note: You cannot yet validate the attendance hours recorded against the work capacity percentage field. You need to add a custom field in Job Info for doing that and then use the time valuation rules.

(Screenshot: Partial attendance during long-term sickness absence)

Overtime on Mandatory Absence Days

Another example is during company shutdowns or mandatory absence days.

Scenario

  • Day is recorded as a full‑day shutdown absence
  • Employee works overtime late in the evening due to critical tasks

Collision handling allows overtime to be recorded outside planned working time, without forcing cancellation or adjustment of the absence.

Overtimes are recorded with time type classifications “Attendance”. Hence, you need a time type grouping with category “Attendance”.

Configuration Used:

Assign the collision grouping to the overtime time type and that’s it.

Overtime can be recorded from 5 pm to 7pm after absence hours with the collision resolution configuration.

(Screenshot: Overtimes after full day absence hours)

Support for Duration-Based Users

The collision handling between Absences and Attendances is not limited to clock‑time–based users. This enhancement also fully supports duration‑based users.

Previously, if an employee had a full‑day absence and recorded attendance on that same day using duration‑based recording, the system would raise a collision error and block the record. With the new enhancement, this limitation is removed. You can now configure the system to allow attendance records even on days with full‑day absences.

This provides much more flexibility, especially for employees who record time in hours rather than start and end times.

Key Points to Remember

  • This is only relevant for full‑day or multi‑day absences
  • Physical time overlaps are not evaluated, as duration‑based users do not record clock times
  • The feature is also applicable for day‑based time recordings
  • Overlapping hours can still be handled appropriately in time valuation, if required

Example with Duration‑Based Recording

Scenario

  • Employee is a day-based recorder
  • Employee has a full‑day absence recorded (for example, Vacation)
  • The same day, the employee records 0,5 day of attendance using duration‑based entry (for example, for an urgent task or on‑call support)

(Screenshot: Duration-based user with attendance days on full day absence)

Integration with Time Valuation

The collision handling between Absences and Attendances does not stop at allowing or restricting overlaps. An equally important aspect is how these overlapping hours are treated during time valuation, especially when overtime (OT) calculations are involved.

In many customer scenarios, overlaps between attendance and absence should not contribute to paid working time or overtime. Instead, only the non‑overlapping attendance hours should be evaluated against overtime rules. The following example demonstrates how this can be achieved.

Business Scenario

Let’s consider the following setup:

  • Standard shift: 09:00 – 17:00 (8 hours, no break)
  • Overtime rule:
    Overtime is generated only if more than 30 minutes are worked beyond the planned shift.
  • Attendance overlapping with absence:
    Overlapping hours are disregarded for both total working time and overtime calculation

In short:

  1. Any overlap between attendance and absence is removed from the attendance duration.
  2. Only the remaining attendance hours are passed to time valuation.
  3. Overtime is calculated based on the cleaned‑up attendance hours.

Example Scenarios:

Partial Leave on First Half of the Day

  • Planned leave: 09:00 – 13:00(first half leave)
  • Clock‑in / Clock‑out: 12:45 – 17:00

Here, 15 minutes of attendance overlap with the absence (12:45 – 13:00).
This overlap is disregarded, resulting in 4:00 hours of valid attendance (13:00 – 17:00).

Since the employee does not exceed the planned shift by more than 30 minutes, no overtime is generated.

Partial Leave on Second Half with Overtime Generation

  • Planned leave: 13:00 – 17:00(Second Half)
  • Clock‑in / Clock‑out: 08:30 – 13:15

In this example:

  • Attendance overlaps with absence for 15 minutes, which is disregarded
  • Valid attendance duration becomes 4:45 hours
  • After disregarding the overlap, the employee still works 8:30 hours in total which is more than 30 minutes beyond the planned shift

As a result, 30 minutes of overtime is generated, while the overlapping 15 minutes are not considered for OT and disregarded.

Configuration in Time Valuation:

First, remove the overlapping hours between absences and attendances and collect the overlap hours in a time type group. I have used the “Deduct Group from Input Group” valuation type.

 

Now, calculate the overtime hours by using the “Aggregate Input Group and Split” method.

Next, you can decide if these overtime hours shall be paid out or not. I have used the valuation type “Compare Threshold with Input Groups and Count Events”. In my rule, only if it is above or equal to 30 mins, it shall be paid out. 

The threshold value can be decided based on your requirements.

Note: The rules in time valuation can also be configured differently. Please consider these examples for illustration purposes only.

Key Takeaway

This integration ensures that:

  • Overlapping attendance hours are not double‑counted
  • Overtime is calculated only on valid working time
  • Payroll and compliance expectations are met consistently

From my experience, this level of control is essential for customers who want to support flexible time recording without unintentionally inflating paid hours or overtime.

By combining collision resolution with time valuation rules, you can precisely control what gets paid and when—even in complex real‑world scenarios.

 

Conclusion

The Absence and Attendance Collision Resolution feature addresses a long‑standing gap between system rules and working reality.

When configured carefully, it enables:

  • Flexible time recording
  • Reliable integrations
  • Better employee and administrator experience

In addition, you have full control over how overlapping attendance hours are handled during time valuation. Depending on your business requirements, you can decide whether these overlapping hours should be considered for payment calculations or excluded entirely. I covered this aspect in more detail in the "Integration with Time Valuation" section of this blog, in case you need guidance for your implementations.

As always, thoughtful solution design, testing, and bidirectional configuration are key. But once this feature is in place, it significantly improves how time is managed in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking, especially in complex, real‑world scenarios.

I hope this blog is insightful and helps you implement Time Tracking faster and with more confidence than ever before! 😊

Feel free to provide feedback or like this blog 🙂

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP Private 2025 Key Innovations in Sourcing and Procurement
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP Private 2025 Key Innovations in Sourcing and Procurement

The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 release for Sourcing and Procurement introduces several valuable enhancements, most notably the new SAP Fiori app “My Purchase Requisitions – New.” This blog provides a concise overview of the new capabilities.

1) Start and End Dates for Service Items

Service items (product type Service) now include Start Date and End Date fields to define the service period, replacing the single Delivery Date field.

This feature enables accurate representation of services as Services are delivered over a period but not on one day. Capturing Start/End Date aligns the requisition with how services are actually performed and consumed.

This feature also allows Improved compliance and control and specifying explicit service periods support policy compliance (e.g., contract validity, budget availability for the period, approval rules tied to dates) and reduce the risk of ordering services outside approved timeframes.

2) Harmonized Document Management (HDM) in “Create Purchase Order – Advanced”

The Create Purchase Order – Advanced app now supports Harmonized Document Management (HDM) for purchase order header-level attachments.

With this release, Harmonized Document Management (HDM) can be used for attachments at purchase order header level. This is SAP’s unified approach to have all the documents at one place instead of several places and across all Fiori apps.

This Fiori app Create Purchase Order-Advanced now allows to attach the documents at header level, as it aligns with SAP’s unified approach to have all documents at one place instead of several places and across all Fiori apps.

3) Aggregation of Lean Service–Related PO History Items

Lean service standard items and enhanced limit items can now be included in ME87 (Summarization and Removal of PO History Records). 

For SAP Lean Services, aggregating Lean Service–related PO History items (e.g., for Service POs tied to SES) creates value mainly by improving control, speed, and transparency across PO → SES → GR/IR → Invoice.

It also delivers other business values in End-to-End visibility for services spend, faster SES and Invoice exception handling, Better Audit trail and compliance are some of them.

However, there are certain restrictions on using the RM06EKBE report:

  • Parked invoices will not be included in the aggregation.
  • Blocked invoices will not be included in the aggregation.
  • Aggregation will not occur if the GR quantity and invoice quantity don’t match.
  • Unapproved SES will not be aggregated.

4) Display Header Attachments for Purchase Requisition Items in “My Inbox”

Approvers using the My Inbox app can now view purchase requisition header-level attachments (from the GOS table) directly when approving PR items—alongside item-level attachments (from the DMS table).

If you approve a purchase requisition item using the My Inbox app, you can now directly display the attachments on header level (located in the GOS table) in addition to the attachments on item level (located in the DMS table) instead of navigating to the header as before.

5) Add PR Items from a Model Product Specification (MPS)

With this Fiori app Manage Purchase Requisitions - Professional, you can now use the Items/Item Sets from MPS option under Create to select and add items or items from an MPS. This option is only available if the configuration has activated the item hierarchy function and you have switched on the Hierarchy List view in the purchase requisition items table.

This feature enables quicker PR creation as Users can generate a complete, multi-line requisition in seconds instead of manually entering items, quantities, and attributes instead of lines by line.

It also reduces cycle time (requisition lead time) from request to approval and purchasing.

  • On selecting an MPS ID and choosing OK, the items and item sets in that MPS are listed. You can then select the items and item sets that you want to add to the item table and choose Add.

  • The selected items and item sets are now added to the purchase requisition items table.

Effects on Configuration

In the standard system, the item hierarchy function is switched off by default. The configuration expert can activate the item hierarchy function using the configuration step Activate Item Hierarchies in Purchasing present under Sourcing and Procurement -->General Settings -->Item Hierarchy. Note that once the item hierarchy function is switched on, it cannot be turned off.

The new features can be accessed from below SAP Help site.

https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5fc51e30e2744f168642e26e0c1d9be1?clear=all&Version=2025&Line_of_Business=Sourcing+and+Procurement&Business_Area=Operational+Procurement

Thank you for reading, and I encourage everyone to embrace the latest innovations that come with SAP S/4HANA!. If you find this blog useful, please consider giving it a "like" or sharing it. I’d love to hear your thoughts and feedback, so feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, just drop them in the Q&A section with the tags SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We'll try to get back to you as soon as we can.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: Reason Field Not available in Partial Inspection Lot in Public CLoud
entry.content:
Hello everyone

I am trying to perform partial inspection. When I try to perform Partial inspection with Follow up function SWM_RP, it doesn't show me reason field to enter any value.

But when I assign follow up function SWM_SCP to Decision code R1, it shows up reason field because it is follow up function of scrap through cost center.

My requirement is user wants to add reason field to rejection code with follow up function SWM_RP. Is it possible through standard? Or I have to go with enhancement and add any any text field?

Note: All these requirements are for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Supply Chain

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My Name is Meghana .D And I Am Exicted to join the SAP
entry.content:
My name is Meghana D, and I am a Mechanical Engineering student with an interest in technology and business systems. I have recently started exploring SAP and its real-world applications. I find SAP interesting as it brings different business processes together in one place. I am really interested in learning more and improving my skills in this area. I would love to be a part of the SAP Community to learn from others and share my ideas.

Thank you SAP Community for this opportunity.

Here are few questions I have chosen  to answer as a part of Hello SAP Community initiative:

1.What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to build a successful career where I can continuously learn and keep growing. I aim to become a skilled professional, work on real-world projects, and make a positive impact through my work.

2. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In the next 5 years, I see myself as a confident and skilled professional, working in a good company. I want to grow in my career, gain experience, and become someone who can handle responsibilities independently.

3. Share a fun fact/story about yourself that people don't know

A fun fact about me is that I enjoy trying new things, even if I’m not sure how to start.
I like learning step by step and figuring things out on my own. Sometimes I challenge myself just to see how much I can improve. I enjoy exploring new ideas, especially in technology. It keeps me curious, motivated, and always learning.

4.What do you imagine or predict is the future of your topic? (AI, Cloud etc., be creative be daring, be entertaining)  AI and cloud will make life easier and smarter. They will help us plan, learn, and do work faster. Cloud will connect everything so we can access data anytime. AI will handle boring tasks, and humans will focus on ideas. We must use it carefully so it helps people in a good way.

5. What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The best lesson my parents taught me is to always stay honest and work hard in everything I do. They also taught me to be patient and never give up, no matter the situation.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025

SAP TechEd Bangalore 2025 was a truly inspiring experience for me. It was exciting to learn about SAP’s AI-driven future and be part of such a vibrant global tech community. This experience motivated me even more to continue learning and growing in my journey as a technology professional.
Attending SAP TechEd Bangalore with my college friends made it even more special. We explored different sessions, visited demo pods, and interacted with SAP experts, which gave me great exposure as a student.
One of the most interesting parts for me was the demo pods, where I saw how SAP BTP is used in real-world scenarios to solve business challenges. It made everything feel more practical, engaging, and exciting.

 Key Takeaways from the event:

1. Generative AI is playing an important role in making business processes smarter and more efficient.
2. The Clean Core approach helps keep systems simple, flexible, and ready for future changes.
3.  Technology is also supporting sustainability by enabling better, data-driven decisions.
4.  A cloud-first approach is helping organizations move faster in their digital transformation journey.
5. Integration plays a key role in connecting different systems and ensuring smooth operations.

 The best part of SAP TechEd 2025 for me was being part of such an energetic and inspiring community. More than the announcements, I really enjoyed the hands-on learning and seeing how AI and data are applied in real-world scenarios

 This learning experience, I explored how SAP enables efficient procurement operations and supports businesses in making data-driven decisions. It also enhanced my knowledge of the SAP Material Management (MM)  and its real-world applications.

Connecting with others, sharing ideas, and experiencing that positive energy made the whole event truly memorable.
Overall, this event was more than just a learning experience—it motivated me to keep growing in the SAP ecosystem. It gave me a fresh perspective, boosted my confidence, and encouraged me to take on new challenges.
It truly inspired me to aim higher and contribute meaningfully to the world of technology.

 I’m happy to share that I have completed the SAP course. Recently, I successfully completed the SAP certification-"Introducing Procurement Processes". This certification helped me gain a deeper understanding of procurement workflows, including purchasing processes, supplier management, and material flow within an organization

This experience helped me to understand how SAP supports businesses with better and smarter procurement processes. I also got basic knowledge of the SAP Material Management (MM) module

Here are the key takeaways

1. Supplier/vendor management basics

2. Inventory and material handling knowledge

3. Introduction to SAP MM (Material Management) module

4. End-to-end procurement process understanding

5. Basics of purchase requisition & purchase order

This is just the beginning, and I am looking forward to learning more advanced SAP modules and concepts in the future.

I would love to connect with fellow leamers and professionals in the SAP Community.

Thank you for your support!

Now it's your turn!

Which technology excites you the most right now, and why?

I would love to hear your thoughts as well.

entry.title: Re: Oauth2 grant_type ''password" is not working after JD...
entry.content:
Dear @chetanks3005 ,

Could you review the below help document for reference and specifically on 8th point b section of impex script.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_COMPOSABLE_STOREFRONT/10a8bc7f635b4e3db6f6bb7880e58a7d/f423b314b3a94ef385b7683707701f8b.html?locale=en-US#updating-composable%20storefront%20to-221121.1 

The following steps are required if you are using composable storefront with SAP Commerce Cloud 2211-jdk21.1. You can ignore these steps if you are using SAP Commerce Cloud 2211.44 or earlier.
In src/app/spartacus/spartacus-features.module.ts, set the authorizationCodeFlowByDefault, incrementProcessesCountForMergeCart, dispatchLoginActionOnlyWhenTokenReceived, and cdsLoginEventsToken feature flags to true. For more information, see Authentication.
Create new OAuth client credentials in SAP Commerce Cloud 2211-jdk21.1.
The credentials must have the public property set to true, and must not include a client secret.

The following is an example ImpEx that matches the default configuration of composable storefront:

# Public OAuth Client Credential
INSERT_UPDATE OAuthClientDetails; clientId[unique=true] ;public ;authorities ;scope ;authorizedGrantTypes ;registeredRedirectUri ;loginPageUri
; mobile_android_public ;true ;ROLE_CLIENT ;basic ;authorization_code,refresh_token ;http://localhost:4200 ;http://localhost:4200/login

 

And also refer the below additional documentation related to new oauth implementation guide.

Moving to the New OAuth Implementation
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_CLOUD_PUBLIC_CLOUD/75d4c3895cb346008545900bffe851ce/d079f886cab647e5a0555e2cae8e4416.html?locale=en-US 

Authentication
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_COMPOSABLE_STOREFRONT/eaef8c61b6d9477daf75bff9ac1b7eb4/7e237cc1245c43219e0a957a2cfa81c2.html?locale=en-US 

https://me.sap.com/notes/3707853  : Example of Oauth Custom Token Granter implementation after JDK21 upgrade
https://me.sap.com/notes/3641255  : Moving to the New OAuth Implementation in Commerce Cloud JDK 21: Client Validation and Migration Considerations
https://me.sap.com/notes/3408108  : OAuth Authorization does not work after Upgrade to 2211.*


Regards,

Prasanth

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: SAP Serial Number Stock Inconsistency: Fix MB52 vs IQ09 Mismatch
entry.content:
Hi @waqas_kandawala ,

Please verify the following and thank you for taking the time to read the blog:

  • Check 1: Check the serial number profile assigned in the Material Master.
  • Check 2: Verify whether the serial numbers are maintained in the delivery.
  • Check 3: Check the serial number status (e.g., ESTO, ECUS, etc.).
  • Check 4: If the serial number is maintained in delivery and the status is ESTO, then review the configuration of serialization procedures SDAU and SDCC in transaction OIS2. The Serial Number Usage should be set to 03 - Obligatory.

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: Hi. Using BWFID_GET_FIGL_ITEM FM . getting special characters in the output. what could be the issue
entry.content:
Hello ,

 

While using the standard SAP BW extractor function module BWFID_GET_FIGL_ITEM, the output internal table shows hash symbols (####) for currency fields such as DMSOL / DMHAB / WRSOL even before any custom processing.

However, when the same fields are explicitly converted using WRITE … TO CHAR logic, the numeric values are retrieved correctly and the file output is accurate.

Can you please confirm whether this #### display in the extractor output is expected SAP standard behavior due to unformatted CURR fields returned by the extractor (display overflow), and that the underlying numeric values are intact and correct?

Also, please confirm that explicit conversion using WRITE (numeric → CHAR) is the correct and recommended SAP approach when consuming this FM outside BW (e.g., for AL11 CSV / interface outputs).

Thanks

Bhuvaneswari R

entry.title: Re: CO_PRODUCT AFTER WORK ORDER ISSUING
entry.content: You cannot add co‑products at posting time with 101. To get correct costs, co‑products must be defined in the BOM before order release, and then posted with 531. If you try to force 101, you’ll end up with inconsistent costing.

entry.title: When using API CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN, how should you pass parameters to this API
entry.content:
We can use program  CMD_MIG_BP_CVI_CREATE_TEST, it can create a new BP by coping already exist BP. And it call API CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN to create/update BP.

For example we want to update table KNVI, but we don't know how to pass parameter to the API CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN, then we can follow below steps

 1.Create a BP with KNVI data 

2.Set break point at Class CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN method MAINTAIN

3. Then execute program CMD_MIG_BP_CVI_CREATE_TEST and give the BP number which get created in step 1. 

4. Then in debug mode, we can gain insights into how parameters are passed
