entry.title: Re: Standard Template for Bulk Upload of Adjusting Entrie...
entry.content:
Good day @MuhammadAmmad_Munir 

Thank you for your question.

I would first separate this into two different use case, the reason is, in Public Cloud the standard tool is different depending on whether you are posting regular adjusting journals or initial migration/opening balances.

Question 1: Could you please confirm the exact standard template name used for bulk upload of journal entries (e.g., via Migration Cockpit, LTMC, or any other tool)?

Answer: For regular bulk adjusting journal entries, the standard too is the Upload General Journal Entries App.

You can upload multiple G/L journal entries from a spreadsheet or CSV file, and the template for uploading is available in the app itself. This you can use for periodic or ad hoc adjustment posting, not Migration Cockpit.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/93bd0b1e72ca4cbcbfb942d4497529f7/4e440b8d8a1e402abd944cdaef09ec80.html?locale=en-US

 

Question 2: What is the exact path or transaction to download this template, especially when working in QAS environment?

Answer: If you are working in Public Cloud, there is no LTMC transaction. You can download the template directly in the Upload General Journal Entries app.  See above.

 

Question 3: Is the same template used for uploading adjusting entries, or is there a separate template specifically for opening balances / migration?

Answer: No, not as the standard best practice. For regular adjusting entries, you use the Upload General Journal Entries.

For Initial migration/opening balances, you use Migration Cockpit with the relevant migration object and its own migration template.

For G/L opening balances, the relevant migration object is typically FI-G/L account balance and open/line item.

 

Question 4: Can this upload process be used only for Income & Expense GL accounts, or can it also handle:

o             CWIP (Capital Work in Progress) entries

o             DWIP (Development Work in Progress) entries

Answer: No. For the journal upload app, there is a high-level limitation to G/L journal entries. It is not restricted to only income statement accounts.

So, in principle, you can use it for balance sheet and P&L G/L posting as long as your posting is a valid G/L journal entry and you also ensure all required accounting assignments are included.

Regarding it handling CWIP and DWIP entries, here i would distinguish carefully:

For normal adjustment posting, if your CWIP/DWIP posting is being posted as a valid G/L journal entry with the necessary account assignments, then the standard upload app can be used in principle.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/49d0f15801b640639e801c36e2936597.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/583f130d246a4c61ab954580fe21787a.html?locale=en-US

For opening balances or asset related legacy migration, if the balances are actually part of fixed asset migration or asset related opening balances, then you should not treat them as a generic G/L opening balances.

I would recommend the following best practice:

  1. Use Upload General Journal Entries for bulk adjustment journals during operations or period end. The template is available inside the app for downloads.
  2. Use Migration Cockpit only for initial migration or opening balances and download the template for the selected migration object there.
  3. Don’t use LTMC as your reference in Public Cloud. The standard approach is apps-based.
  4. For asset related balances, including cases where WIP is effectively asset migration content, make use of the relevant fixed asset migration object rather than the G/L balance object.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: The Silent Drain: How Duplicate Payments Slip Through SAP and How to Stop Them
entry.content:
Introduction

Duplicate payments can cost organizations millions every year.
Even with SAP’s built-in duplicate invoice checks, overpayments still occur. This blog outlines the existing gaps and provides effective solutions to address them. The key question is, given that SAP already includes duplicate invoice checks, how do companies still end up paying the same invoice twice? In today's invoice processing landscape, where invoices flow through multiple systems such as Ariba, OCR tools, and automated interfaces, duplicate payments are not rare exceptions. They occur more often than expected. The real challenge is not the absence of controls, but how effectively they are implemented and maintained. SAP can prevent duplicate invoices but only when standard functionality, configuration, and integrations are properly aligned.

Why Duplicate Invoice Checks Became Critical in Projects

In one of my projects, we discovered instances of vendors receiving multiple payments for the same invoice.

At first, it appeared to be an isolated issue. However, after further analysis, we realized that duplicate invoices were getting posted and processed for payment even though SAP standard duplicate checks were active. This created several challenges, like financial loss due to overpayments, increased effort to recover payments from vendors, audit and compliance concerns, and more workload for the Accounts Payable team. When we analyzed the root cause, we found that the issue was not with SAP capability but with how invoice data was processed and validated across systems.

The overall process control, not just system configuration, determines the effectiveness of duplicate invoice prevention.

Standard Duplicate Invoice Check in SAP

In SAP, there is already a standard mechanism available to identify duplicate invoices at the time of posting. This acts as the first level of control to prevent unintended overpayments.
During invoice posting, the system checks for duplicates based on a combination of key fields, such as company code, vendor, invoice number (reference – XBLNR), invoice date, and invoice amount. This validation is triggered in common transactions like MIRO for PO-based invoices and FB60 for non-PO invoices.
Based on configuration, SAP can either issue a warning and allow the user to proceed or raise an error and block the posting entirely. In most business scenarios, configuring this check as an error provides stronger control and helps avoid duplicate postings at the source.
However, in real project scenarios, I have observed that this standard functionality, while effective, does not always prevent duplicates completely.

The effectiveness of this standard functionality heavily relies on the maintenance of certain aspects, such as:

  • Accuracy and consistency of the invoice reference (XBLNR)
  • Proper maintenance of vendor master data
  • Correct configuration of duplicate check settings

This stage is where most real-world gaps begin to appear, especially in integrated and automated environments.

T-Code: OMRDC
Path: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Materials Management -> Logistics Invoice Verification -> Incoming Invoice -> Set Check for Duplicate Invoices
Duplicate Invoice Check Configuration at Company Code Level

This configuration controls how SAP reacts when a duplicate invoice is detected.
In this case, the system is set to check invoice reference and invoice date, which helps strengthen duplicate validation.

MM vs FI Duplicate Invoice Checks

One of the key lessons I learned from my experience was that duplicate invoice checks in SAP do not behave the same way for all types of invoices. At a high level, SAP uses similar fields for validation. But in reality, the way duplicate checks work for MM (PO-based) and FI (non-PO) invoices is quite different, and this is where many issues begin.

MM Invoices (PO-Based – MIRO)

For PO-based invoices, SAP follows the three-way match process (PO–GR–Invoice). Because of this, the system does not rely only on the invoice number it also considers the invoice amount along with defined tolerance limits. In one of the scenarios I worked on, we noticed that even when the same invoice number was used, the invoice was still getting posted. The reason was that the amount difference was within the configured tolerance. This means that in MM, invoice amount plays a significant role, whereas tolerance settings directly influence duplicate detection.
As a result, duplicate invoices can still pass if the differences fall within acceptable limits.

Path: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide -> Materials Management -> Logistics Invoice Verification -> Incoming Block -> Set Tolerance Limits

Tolerance settings for PP influencing duplicate invoice behavior in MIRO

 

Tolerance settings for DQ influencing duplicate invoice behavior in MIRO

FI Invoices (Non-PO – FB60)

For non-PO invoices, the validation works differently. Here, SAP relies more on accounting-level checks, where the primary dependency is on the invoice reference (XBLNR) and vendor. In practice, I’ve seen cases where small variations in the invoice number, such as adding a hyphen or suffix, allowed duplicate invoices to pass through without any error. From a business perspective, both represent the same invoice. But from a system perspective, they are treated as different. This makes FI postings more sensitive to data consistency rather than amount validation.

Understanding this difference is critical in real-world projects. In MM, duplicates may pass due to tolerance flexibility. In FI, duplicates may pass due to invoice number variations. This is one of the main reasons why duplicate payments still occur even when standard SAP checks are enabled.
From my experience, duplicate invoice issues are rarely caused by missing configuration. They are usually caused by tolerance settings not aligned with business expectations, inconsistent invoice reference handling, and differences in how MM and FI validations are applied. Recognizing this gap is the first step toward improving duplicate invoice control.

Impact of Customization and Invoice Processing

In real projects, invoices don’t come only through MIRO or FB60. They flow from systems like Ariba, OCR tools, and interfaces. This is where duplicate checks start breaking. Even with proper SAP configuration, duplicates can still pass due to a few common gaps.

Where Things Go Wrong

One of the most common issues is invoice number variation.
For example, INV123 and INV-123 represent the same invoice for the business, but SAP treats them as different. Another major gap is incorrect field mapping. If the invoice number is not correctly updated in XBLNR, the duplicate check simply won’t work. In some cases, the same invoice is processed through multiple channels like Ariba and manual entry. SAP cannot always detect duplicates across these parallel flows. Custom developments can also impact validation. Enhancements, BADIs, or custom programs may bypass standard checks, weakening the control. On top of this, tolerance settings in MM and weaker validation in FI can allow duplicates to pass within acceptable limits.

Enhancements and Tools to Strengthen Duplicate Invoice Detection

SAP standard checks provide a strong foundation. But in real-world scenarios, they are not enough. To truly control duplicates, additional measures are required. From my experience, a few practical improvements make a big difference. First, standardizing invoice numbers is critical. Even small variations like INV123 and INV-123 can bypass checks, so consistency is key. Next, validating data before it reaches SAP helps prevent duplicates at the source. Interface-level checks ensure incorrect or duplicate invoices don’t even enter the system. Another effective approach is fuzzy matching, which helps identify similar invoice numbers and catch near-duplicates that standard SAP logic might miss.

In integrated environments, cross-system validation becomes important. Comparing invoices across SAP, Ariba, and OCR systems ensures the same invoice isn’t processed through different channels. Finally, strengthening validation in FI, especially around invoice reference handling, reduces risks from manual entry.

To enhance SAP capabilities, organizations often use:

  • OpenText VIM – Advanced invoice management with validation
  • SAP Ariba Invoice Management – Upstream control and validation
  • Kofax / ReadSoft – OCR with data validation capabilities
  • BlackLine – AP automation and reconciliation
  • Celonis – Process mining to identify duplicate patterns

Business Impact: How Much Can Be Saved?

Duplicate payments are not just system issues they represent direct financial leakage and operational inefficiency. Even a small percentage of duplicate invoices can result in significant monetary loss, especially in high-volume accounts payable environments. With proper SAP configuration, organizations can typically reduce duplicates by 60–70%. By adding the enhancements discussed above, this can improve further to around 85–95% prevention.

Conclusion

SAP provides strong standard functionality to detect duplicate invoices. However, in real projects, duplicates still occur not because SAP is lacking, but because controls are not consistently applied across the process. In my experience, preventing duplicates is not just about configuration. It depends on how well data, integrations, and processes are aligned. While SAP can prevent a large portion of duplicates, achieving better accuracy requires additional validations and continuous monitoring. Duplicate invoice prevention is not a single control it is an end-to-end process.

entry.title: Re: Reason Field Not available in Partial Inspection Lot...
entry.content:
You cannot enable the reason field for SWM_RP through customizing in Public Cloud. SAP has intentionally limited which follow‑up functions show the reason field. The “reason” field is tied to specific follow‑up functions where SAP expects a justification (scrap, write‑off). For return postings (SWM_RP), the system assumes the return is self‑explanatory and does not expose the reason field.

If the business requires a reason for returns, you would need to use In‑App Extensibility (Custom Fields and Logic) to add a custom text field and include it in the inspection decision UI. Instead of modifying SWM_RP, consider using standard fields like inspection code or usage decision to capture rejection reasons. This keeps the solution upgrade‑safe.

entry.title: SAP Contract and Lease Mgmt Product & strategy updates: latest innovation and available content
entry.content:
SAP ERP Cloud addresses these challenges with its Contract & Lease Management (CLM) capabilities. This solution provides a centralized, automated approach to managing the entire lease lifecycle - from contract creation and valuation to accounting and reporting - while ensuring compliance with international standards like IFRS 16 and US GAAP ASC 842.

With features such as automated lease valuation, sublease and intercompany handling, sales-based rent management, and embedded analytics, CLM empowers businesses to streamline operations, reduce risk, and gain actionable insights—all in a cloud-native environment designed for scalability and agility.

Prior beginning to list the latest releases it is necessary to briefly explain the scope items that currently part of contract and lease management in public cloud

Please find below a structure of the currently available scope items.

 

Lease-In Accounting: Managing Leases You Use

Lease-In Accounting focuses on scenarios where your organization is the lessee—in other words, when you rent or lease assets such as office buildings, vehicles, or equipment.

This capability supports the financial accounting requirements of leased assets, ensuring compliance with modern lease accounting standards. Typical activities include managing right-of-use assets, tracking lease liabilities, and posting depreciation and interest expenses over the life of a lease.

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, these processes are enabled through scope items 1T6, 21P, and 34N.

Lease-Out and Sublease Accounting: Leasing Assets to Others

Lease-Out Accounting covers the opposite scenario—when your company acts as the lessor and leases assets to external parties. This capability also includes subleasing, where a leased asset is further leased to another party.

Here, the focus is on managing lease contracts, recognizing lease revenue, and correctly accounting for sublease arrangements. It ensures that both operational and financial aspects of outgoing leases are handled consistently.

Relevant SAP scope items for this capability include 34O, 2SA, 2SB, and 2O9.

 

Intercompany Processes

For organizations with multiple legal entities, Intercompany Processes address lease and contract scenarios that occur within the same corporate group.

These processes support intercompany lease agreements, internal billing, and financial eliminations required for group reporting. By handling these transactions in a structured way, SAP helps maintain transparency while avoiding double-counting in consolidated financial statements.

This capability is supported by SAP scope items 7G5, 7G4, and 7EZ.

 

Additional CLM Capabilities: Supporting and Specialized Scenarios

Not all contract and lease management scenarios fit neatly into a single category. The “Various” capability brings together supporting and specialized CLM functionalities that enhance the overall solution.

This includes reporting, system integrations, special contract scenarios, and other financial processes that complement core lease accounting. These capabilities ensure flexibility and completeness across diverse business needs.

SAP scope items associated with this area include 7AD, 6N8, 3F4, 21R, and 77V.

 

After walking through the overall structure of Contract and Lease Management (CLM) in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, this blog turns its focus to what’s new. Over the past two years, SAP has delivered a series of meaningful enhancements that strengthen financial transparency. Below is a practical overview of the most important feature releases and what they bring to customers.

 

Release 2402: Better Financial Insight and Stronger Integration

With Release 2402, SAP introduced the Lease and Real Estate Accounting Review Booklet app, giving finance teams an easier way to compare planned versus actual costs across both real estate and non–real estate portfolio objects. The ability to create customizable review booklets makes it much simpler to analyze financial performance and support informed decision-making.

At the same time, SAP enhanced the integration with Planon Real Estate Management for SAP. These improvements focus on accounting efficiency by supporting accounting-specific configurations, automated cost allocation notifications, and journal entry postings through APIs and dedicated SAP apps. The result is a smoother, more connected experience across real estate, asset management, and lease accounting processes.

 

Release 2408: Intercompany Lease Processing Comes to CLM

Release 2408 marked an important step forward with the introduction of Intercompany Lease Processing in Contract and Lease Management. This capability allows companies within the same corporate group to manage real estate leases between legal entities in a structured and transparent way.

Organizations can now handle intercompany lease-in and lease-out contracts, perform periodic accounts receivable and payable postings, and run periodic valuations for these contracts. By clearly defining rights and responsibilities between entities, this feature helps reduce complexity, improve operational efficiency, and strengthen internal governance.

 

Release 2502: Deeper Accounting Integration with Planon

In Release 2502, SAP continued to build on the Planon integration, with a strong focus on improving the accounting user experience. New enhancements enable accounting-specific configurations and more automated cost allocation processes.

Key highlights include the introduction of the Accounting Business Transaction Interface, which allows organizations to define expense types and account determination rules. Journal entries can now be posted directly using the OData V4 API, based on real estate cost allocation notifications. In addition, the Manage Accounting Notification app provides detailed reference information, helping users clearly understand and track accounting-relevant events. Together, these improvements ensure a seamless connection between Planon and SAP S/4HANA.

 

Release 2508: A More Flexible Cost Settlement Process

The latest enhancement in Release 2508 introduces a new, more flexible cost settlement process for real estate and contract management. Costs can now be allocated using a hierarchy based on architectural structures, usable or rentable objects, and CLM contracts.

This process relies on several new and enhanced apps, such as Manage Service Groups, Manage Contract Settlement Regulations, Synchronize Cost Settlement Related Items, and Classify Journal Entry Items. These tools make it easier to classify invoices and journal entries and settle costs accurately and efficiently. Overall, this release significantly improves both efficiency and usability for organizations managing complex real estate and lease scenarios.

 

If you’d like, I can also help tailor this for a customer-facing SAP blog, shorten it for a release highlights post, or align it with a specific industry perspective (for example, real estate, retail, or manufacturing).

 

Release 2602:

New Cost Settlement Apps for Contract and Lease Management

Two new apps enhance the cost settlement process in Contract and Lease Management. The Manage Cost Settlements (F7391) app enables users to create, edit, and simulate cost settlements. The Display Results of Cost Settlement Process Steps (F9079) app provides detailed insights into the results of settlements triggered in the Manage Cost Settlements app.

Value Proposition

  • Improve efficiency and tenant satisfaction by accurately allocating service costs.
  • Ensure reliable settlement of real estate–related service costs based on predefined criteria.

Capabilities

  • Settle service costs to tenants based on buildings or land, space participation in cost categories, rentable units, and rental contracts.
  • Simulate settlements and analyse detailed results such as apportionment factors, apportionable costs, and cost distribution to rentable objects.

 

Display Account Balance (New Version) – F8301

The Display Account Balance (New Version) (F8301) app allows users to view account balances using flexible filters such as business partner or contract account. A tab-based layout provides quick access to account balances, payment history, dunning activities, and business transactions.

Additional information—such as processing locks, instalment plans, dispute cases, security deposits, and returns—is displayed as buttons with item counts for quick insight. The Show Timeline feature displays all related business transactions with sorting and filtering options. The redesigned UI enables faster access to key account information, helping users resolve inquiries quickly and improving usability with a modern, intuitive interface.

Capabilities

  • Flexible filters to display account balances
  • Tab-based view of account balance, payment history, dunning activities, and transactions
  • Contextual buttons for locks, installment plans, disputes, deposits, and returns
  • Timeline view with sorting and filtering options
  • Modern UI for faster navigation

Source: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Finance | SAP Community

 

This blog will be updated regularly and will serve as a central reference. It summarizes the latest release highlights in a clear and comprehensive way, while also maintaining a record of highlights from previous releases.

 

For further guidance on structuring content, please refer to the recently published blog post. This blog complements the guidance and follows the proposed structure.
Driving adoption by structuring the current availa... - SAP Community

This blog was co-authored with @SCH1 (Janine Schneider)

 

 

#ContractandLease #Finance

entry.title: Re: Mass Deletion of PRs with Deleted PO Items (S/4HANA P...
entry.content:
Good day @shubham1261 

Thank you for your question.

The clean core approach I would recommend is for you to close PR items that you no longer require, rather than relying on the PO deletion to clean them up automatically. Note that “closed” field cannot be mass changed in the standard PR mass change app. You can use API_PURCHASEREQ_PROCESS_SRV with IsClosed for bulk updates. For smaller volumes, use Manage Purchase Requisitions-Professional and manually set the PR item to Closed.

Your next step should be for you to decide whether your volume is low enough for app based manual closure or whether you need a released API based bulk cleanup process. Also test workflow behaviour because based on how your organisation has designed its workflow, changing the “Closed” flag can restart workflow in some cases.

Here are references for more guidance

2958312 - How to mass update Purchase Requisitions to Closed - SAP for Me

3642394 - How to clear the open commitment for Purchase Requisition - SAP for Me

3288094 - Restart of Flexible Workflow For Rejected or In approval documents - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

When i confirm the production order with auto GR by CO11N,   i have 2 situations as below,

1. If the output material GR storage is set no WM storage, it could generate the batch automatically with the production order confirmation synchronously.

2. If the output material GR storage is set WM storage, it could not generate the batch automatically with the production order confirmation synchronously. I only could create the batch in the app 'Change inbound deliveries' , and then process GR in the same app ''Change inbound deliveries' .

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: CO11N with auto GR can not generate batch automatically
entry.content:
For WM‑managed storage locations, the system requires inbound delivery processing. That’s why batch creation happens only in the Change Inbound Deliveries app or via MIGO, not directly in CO11N.

The clean‑core options are either to use HU management or inbound delivery processing. If the business insists on synchronous batch creation, you’d need an enhancement via In‑App Extensibility, but that deviates from SAP’s standard WM design.

entry.title: Re: Step-by-Step: Calling S/4HANA Private Cloud Standard OData APIs with OAuth 2.0 from Client Tool
entry.content:
✅ Reply to Naomi0822

Hi @Naomi0822 ,

Good question.
OA2C_CONFIG is used when S/4HANA acts as an OAuth client (outbound calls).
In this blog, S/4HANA acts as the OAuth authorization server for inbound calls.

For inbound Client Credentials flow:

  • OAuth Client ID = SU01 communication user ID
  • Client Secret = SU01 user password
  • The mapping is defined in SOAUTH2

So using SU01 here is by design, not a workaround.

✅ Reply to douglasFarcic

H @douglasFarcic ,

If you use SAP API Management (BTP), it usually doesn’t make sense to customize OAuth per consumer on the S/4HANA backend.

In that architecture:

  • API Management is the OAuth client
  • S/4HANA trusts only a technical communication user
  • Supplier/user control is handled in API Management, not via SAP users

So you don’t need one SAP user per supplier.

For internal or same‑intranet access, however, direct OAuth 2.0 (Client Credentials) on S/4HANA is still a valid and recommended approach.

entry.title: Re: MRP indicator (1 or 2) for more than 1 scheduling agr...
entry.content:
No, you cannot set MRP relevance indicator (1 or 2) for more than one scheduling agreement for the same material/vendor combination in standard S/4HANA Public Cloud. Only one scheduling agreement can be considered MRP‑relevant at a time. If you try to assign multiple, MRP will not know which agreement to consume requirements against. Using MRP Areas can help segregate requirements, but they do not override this restriction.

To meet the requirement, use MRP Areas to segregate planning and assign agreements per area. This keeps the setup clean and avoids inconsistencies.

If your system is on-premise or private cloud, then you would have other options.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Error "/SCWM/L3690: Enter a destination HU" during RF...
entry.content:
Good day @DahanChoi 

Thank you for your question.

The /SCWM/L3692 Enter a destination HU error you see, often appears when the warehouse task is handled as an HU based putaway, and the destination storage type or process requires a destination handling unit. KBA 3295867 addresses that case.

So, based on your comparison between the standard starter warehouse storage type S001 and your custom storage type ZRM01, the most like explanation is:

S001 has a storage type or storage process setting that makes destination HU entry mandatory, while ZRM01 does not. This is not usually caused by RF itself. It is usually caused by the destination storage type definition, or by storage control/HU handling logic attached to that standard storage type.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/e4e0a2dbf30a417c9f3d67724608a04f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/ed17e3800846402e8b1da3f0176d2a67.html?locale=en-US

So, the SAP backend explanation for that error is this:

  • The task is being processed as an HU warehouse task
  • The destination storage type in the starter warehouse is configured so that the stock or HU must be placed into a destination HU
  • Therefore RF confirmation asks for a destination HU, and if none is entered, the system raises /SCWM/L3690. See KBA 3295867.

So, the two likely configuration area behind that are HU requirement at the destination storage type and standard storage control in the starter warehouse.

To answer the question on which specific configuration in the standard warehouse is triggering the error? the most likely trigger is the destination storage type S001 is configured so that Hus are required at the destination, or it is part of a standard putaway process that requires confirmation into a destination HU. See KBA 3295867 for more insights.

I would therefore not start with comparing RF profile. I would start with the setting for:

  • Define Storage Types for S001 and ZRM01
  • The HU requirement/HU managed behaviour
  • Any storage control you may have assigned
  • The relevant warehouse process type you use in the putaway.

So, your next step should be for you to compare S001 and ZRM01 in Define Storage Types, make sure you focus on HU handling, putaway control, and any attached storage control. Then you can repeat the RF test and manually enter a destination HU.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/e8a30c35c57f4391a22aa6102fa3be24.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Enable associated entities as a read-only navigation target
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.

Business software is a tightly integrated network. Application data, configuration data and system data form a giant relational network.
This blog explains how to enable associated entities as a read-only navigation target in the CUBCO app. This can be used, for example, to integrate a where-used list into the configuration app to visualize part of the relational network.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP BTP ABAP environment (2605)
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (2025, SAP Note 3694141, SAP UI5 1.136.12)

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

In this simplified scenario, there is one configuration table for company codes and one configuration table for plants. Each plant is assigned to a company code. Our goal is to display on the object page of a company code all plants to which this company code is assigned. For both tables we use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : zdemo_cc_id not null;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}
@EndUserText.label : 'Plant'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_plant {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key plant_id          : abap.numc(3) not null;
  @AbapCatalog.foreignKey.keyType : #KEY
  @AbapCatalog.foreignKey.screenCheck : true
  company_code          : zdemo_cc_id
    with foreign key zdemo_cc
      where client = zdemo_plant.client
        and company_code = zdemo_plant.company_code;
  country               : land1;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Create read-only navigation target

Create a new projection view entity for entity plant without a behavior definition so the associated entities cannot be edited. Do not define associations to other editable RAP BOs to keep draft, transport, and concurrency handling separate and correct.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Plant'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZC_PLANT
  as select from ZI_Plant
{
      .identification: [ {
        position: 1
      } ]
      .lineItem: [ {
        position: 1
      } ]
      .facet: [ {
        id: 'Plant',
        purpose: #STANDARD,
        type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
        label: 'Plant',
        position: 1
      } ]
  key PlantId,
      CompanyCode,
      .identification: [ {
      position: 2
      } ]
      .lineItem: [ {
        position: 2
      } ]
      Country
}

Access Control:

@EndUserText.label: 'Mapping Role for ZC_Plant'
@MappingRole: true
define role ZC_PLANT {
  grant
    select
      on
        ZC_PLANT
          where
            inheriting conditions from entity ZI_Plant;           
}

Add the association to the company code entity:

@EndUserText.label: 'Company Code'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #MANDATORY
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view entity ZI_CompanyCode
  as select from zdemo_cc
  association to parent ZI_CompanyCode_S as _CompanyCodeAll on $projection.SingletonID = _CompanyCodeAll.SingletonID
  association of many to many ZC_PLANT as _Plant on $projection.CompanyCode = _Plant.CompanyCode
{
  key company_code as CompanyCode,
  @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
  last_changed_at as LastChangedAt,
  @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  local_last_changed_at as LocalLastChangedAt,
  @Consumption.hidden: true
  1 as SingletonID,
  _CompanyCodeAll,
  _Plant
}

Add a new facet to the metadata extension of company code:

@Metadata.layer: #CUSTOMER
@UI: {
  headerInfo: {
    typeName: 'CompanyCode', 
    typeNamePlural: 'CompanyCodes', 
    title: {
      type: #STANDARD, 
      label: 'Company Code', 
      value: 'CompanyCode'
    }
  }
}
annotate view ZI_CompanyCode with
{
  .identification: [ {
    position: 1 
  } ]
  .lineItem: [ {
    position: 1 
  } ]
  .facet: [ {
    id: 'CompanyCode', 
    purpose: #STANDARD, 
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE, 
    label: 'Company Code', 
    position: 1 
  },
  {
    id: 'Plant', 
    type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE, 
    label: 'Plant', 
    position: 2 , 
    targetElement: '_Plant'
  } ]
  CompanyCode;
}

Add the plant entity to the service definition of company code:

@ObjectModel.leadingEntity.name: 'ZI_CompanyCode_S'
define service ZUI_COMPANYCODE {
  expose ZI_CompanyCode_S as CompanyCodeAll;
  expose ZI_CompanyCode   as CompanyCode;
  expose ZC_PLANT         as Plant;
}

To enable the user to navigate from the object page of a company code to the object page of a plant, add a row for the plant entity in the "Table Settings" section of the BC maintenance object of the company code.

The company code object page now displays all plants that are assigned to this company code. While the user can edit the company code, the list of plants is not editable.

Intent navigation

The next step to improve the application is to annotate the Plant field with an intent navigation to the Custom Business Configurations app. This allows the user to navigate from the company code object page to the maintenance app of a specific plant.

Currently, this requires the creation of a custom navigation target as it is not possible to use a constant in the semanticObjectBinding annotation. 

If you think this type of intent navigation is important to your application, you can vote and comment on this improvement request: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/338756/?section=sectionDetails

entry.title: Re: Spool Status Montiroting via Cloud ALM
entry.content:

Do you mean the spool request status monitoring in SP01, SP02, SPAD?

Best regards,
Ellie

entry.title: Re: Joule A2A: Connect Code Based Agents into Joule
entry.content:

entry.title: Re: QM01 Notification Item Overview defect description LO...
entry.content:
Dear @inamullah,

No in standard Public Cloud option to add a separate Defect Description LOV / search help directly in the QM01 quality notification item overview table without using the delivered extensibility path for the notification item. SAP documented model for extending quality notification apps is in-app extensibility and SAP separately documents that custom fields can be shown in the relevant quality notification worklist apps. SAP also documents that custom fields can be created as Code List or Code List based on CDS View, which is the standard way to provide an LOV-style value list

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/acb2907f362944a5a9e1b5eb86e769dc.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/46632054fa9d49a18b452ee287173aba.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/c2449b9951d6448aab185c7cebd38539.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/b5e2dbebafdc4cd7807e06e0a5e66347.html

The supported workaround:

  • use the standard defect code/group logic for structured entry and search, or
  • create an item custom field with Code List / CDS-based Code List and expose it where supported, or
  • use the standard Manage Defects / defect search flow where SAP documents filtering and navigation to notification items

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/778cc4e03dbd4b8989278b4ff275b744.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/aa06f52aede74b99926ed4d718fc2823.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Veera Venkata Satyanarayana, an Aspiring Mechanical Engineer Student
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

Moving to Bangalore was a major turning point for me. Seeing the rapid pace of technological evolution in this city sparked a realization: the future isn't just about hardware or software—it’s about how they work together.

Currently, I am a 3rd-year Mechanical Engineering student at Reva University. I’ve dedicated my time to mastering CAD (Catia V5) and simulation (Ansys), but I’ve also expanded my horizon into Python, full-stack web development, and AI. My goal is to apply these digital tools to solve real-world physical problems.

The Recently I Participated in a Hardware Hackathon at New Horizon college of Engineering,  Project: Autonomous Patient Request Receiver
Recently, my team and I participated in a hackathon where we built an Autonomous Patient Request Receiver. This was a deep dive into the practical side of robotics.


The Hardware: We used IR sensors for precision line-following and ultrasonic sensors for obstacle detection.

The Challenge: Our biggest hurdle was the "line-following debug" phase—ensuring the robot could handle tight turns and maintain stability under different lighting conditions.

Next Steps: While I’m currently experimenting with Agent Workflows (and learning from a few failed attempts!), I plan to document my progress and share a detailed technical breakdown in my next post.

Getting to Know Me

1. Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

  • Global Impact: Work on large-scale engineering projects in a tech-forward global environment.

  • Social Entrepreneurship: Build a sustainable business that positively impacts my local community.

  • Continuous Mastery: Reach a level of expertise where I can consult on complex "Physical AI" systems.

  • Innovation: Contribute to breakthroughs in automation and robotics that change how industries operate.

  • Quality of Life: Build a career that offers both a "comfort zone" for my family and high-level professional challenges.

2. What is the best lesson your parents taught you? Persistence and Untold Love. My parents taught me how to sustain myself through "hard flows." They showed me that when you get stuck, you don't stop—you build a new path. Beyond the lessons in critical thinking, they gave me an untold love—a foundation of selfless support that gives me the confidence to take risks and climb toward greater heights.

3. What do you imagine is the future of your topic? I believe the future lies in “Physical AI.” We have seen AI excel in handling data and text, but the next evolution is AI interacting seamlessly with the physical world through robotics and edge computing. I envision a future where platforms like SAP not only manage enterprise data but connect directly with autonomous systems on factory floors—enabling "dark warehouses" and real-time industrial decision-making.

4. Where do you see yourself in 5 years? I see myself in a leading technical role at a global MNC, bridging the gap between mechanical design and enterprise-level automation software.

SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience:

I would like to sincerely thank Reva University and the Mechanical Department for giving me the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore.

Before attending, I had limited clarity about SAP. However, the event completely changed my perspective. I understood the evolution of SAP and its global impact across industries.

One of the most memorable moments was interacting with the SAP Community team and meeting Moshe Naveh. That interaction was a turning point for me.

I realized that SAP is much more than just ERP—it is a powerful ecosystem for automation, data management, and intelligent enterprise solutions.

Key Takeaways:

  1. Clear understanding of SAP’s role in digital transformation
  2. Awareness of SAP Learning resources and Certification paths
  3. Inspiration to explore SAP technologies as part of my career journey

now let’s hear from you in the comments section!

What skill do you believe is most important to stay relevant in the era of AI and automation?

entry.title: Handling Parquet Files in SAP Build Process Automation – Looking for Guidance
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

In our integration design, Parquet files are generated and serve as the input for SAP Build Process Automation (BPA). The requirement document specifies that BPA should pick up these files, process them into SAP, and manage retries, failures, and reconciliation.

So far, BPA workflows have been straightforward with formats like PDF, PNG, JPG, and XLSX. But with Parquet, the challenge is that BPA’s scripting environment doesn’t natively support parsing this format.

My question: What is the recommended way to handle Parquet files in SAP BPA?
Is there a supported method to parse Parquet directly inside BPA?

I’d appreciate insights, references, or experiences from anyone who has addressed this scenario.

Thanks,
Yashmin Behera

SAP Build Process Automation 
SAP Build 

@25shahindrajit25 
@Joanna_Drinnan1 
@Dan_Wroblewski 
@shrinivasan_neelamegam 
@Yogananda
